step_by_step_thinking:
The patient's complaint of pain during swallowing, along with an observation of over-extension in the distolingual area suggests that the superior pharyngeal constrictor muscles may be affected or irritated by the poorly fitting dentures. This muscle group plays a crucial role in elevating the base of the tongue to initiate swallowing. Over-extended soft tissue in this region could lead to discomfort when attempting to swallow food or liquids.

Given these symptoms, I would select option A as it best matches the anatomical location described (distolingual).

The patient's complaint of pain during swallowing, along with an observation of over-extension in the distolingual area suggests that the superior pharyngeal constrictor muscles may be affected or irritated by the poorly fitting dentures. This muscle group plays a crucial role in elevating the base of the tongue to initiate swallowing. Over-extended soft tissue in this region could lead to discomfort when attempting to swallow food or liquids.

Given these symptoms, I would select option A as it best matches the anatomical location described (distolingual).

My Answer: **A** Superi...
My Answer: **A** Superior constrictor. Given the description of pain on swallowing and overextension specifically noted in the distolingual area, which corresponds well with irritation or pressure from improperly fitted dentures against the superior pharyngeal constrictors, my selection aligns precisely with option A. The superior constrictor muscles elevate the posterior wall of the pharynx and play a key role in initiating swallowing; therefore they can become painful if compressed due to ill-fitting dental appliances.
